allopack.title: Fractions on the Number Line — Finding the Space Between
allopack.author: AlloFlow community catalog (authored by Claude, reviewed by A. Pomeranz)
allopack.standards: CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.3.NF.A.2 (understand a fraction as a number on the number line); CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.3.NF.A.3 (explain equivalence of fractions and compare fractions); CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.4.NF.A.1 (explain why fractions are equivalent)

data.body:
**Due:** (your teacher will tell you)

Most people learn fractions as pizza slices. Today you learn them as **places** — spots on a number line. This is the version that keeps working when the math gets bigger.

1. Read **The Space Between 0 and 1**.
2. Learn the words in the **Glossary**, then win the **Word Scramble**.
3. Do the **Practice Problems** one at a time. Say each answer out loud before checking.
4. Sort the cards in **Bigger, Smaller, or Equal to One-Half?**
5. Take the quiz — question 3 catches almost everyone.

Tip: if a fraction confuses you, draw the number line. Every single time. Drawing is not cheating; it is what mathematicians actually do. ✏️📏

objectives.label: I drew a number line to solve at least one problem

history.title: The Space Between 0 and 1
history.data:
## The Space Between 0 and 1

Count out loud: 1, 2, 3, 4. Easy. Now count what is *between* 1 and 2.

There is a whole world in there, and fractions are how we name the places inside it.

Start with a number line. Put 0 at the left and 1 at the right. Now cut the space between them into **4 equal pieces**. The cuts land on new places, and each place has a name: one-fourth, two-fourths, three-fourths. Written as numbers: 1/4, 2/4, 3/4.

The bottom number — the **denominator** — tells you *how many equal pieces the whole was cut into*. The top number — the **numerator** — tells you *how many of those pieces you counted from zero*.

So 3/4 is not a pizza. It is a **location**. Walk three of the four steps from 0 toward 1 and stop. That is where 3/4 lives.

Here is where the number line earns its keep. Look at 2/4. Now look at 1/2. They land on the exact same spot. Same location, two names. That is what **equivalent fractions** means — different words, same place, like a friend who goes by a nickname.

The number line also fixes the most common fraction mistake. Which is bigger, 1/4 or 1/8? Many people say 1/8, because 8 is bigger than 4. But look: cutting the space into 8 pieces makes each piece *smaller*. So 1/8 sits closer to 0, and 1/4 is bigger. **More pieces means smaller pieces.** The number line shows you that instantly, and it never lies.

One more thing. Fractions do not stop at 1. Keep going: 5/4 is one step past 1. Fractions are just numbers, and the number line goes on forever in both directions. Once you see fractions as places instead of slices, they stop being a special weird topic and become ordinary numbers — which is exactly what they are.

data.def: A number that names a place between whole numbers.
The top number — how many equal pieces you counted.
The bottom number — how many equal pieces the whole was cut into.
Equal in value — two fractions that land on the same spot.
The complete amount, from 0 to 1 on the number line.
The same size or the same amount.
One single piece — like 1/4 is one unit of a fourth.
The two equal pieces you get by cutting a whole in two.
The four equal pieces you get by cutting a whole into four.
To decide which of two numbers is bigger, smaller, or equal.

problems.question: A number line runs from 0 to 1 and is cut into 4 equal pieces. Which fraction sits at the very first cut mark after 0?

steps.explanation: The denominator is 4 because the whole was cut into 4 equal pieces.
The first mark after 0 is one piece from the start, so the numerator is 1.

problems.realWorld: A running lap divided into four quarters: the first quarter mark.

problems.question: Which is bigger: 1/4 or 1/8? Explain using pieces.

steps.explanation: Cutting the same whole into 8 pieces makes each piece smaller than cutting it into 4.
So one piece out of 8 is closer to zero than one piece out of 4.

problems.realWorld: Sharing one candy bar with 4 friends versus 8 friends — bigger group, smaller share.

problems.question: Name a fraction that lands on the exact same spot as 1/2 when the line is cut into fourths.

steps.explanation: Cut 0 to 1 into 4 equal pieces and walk 2 of them.
You stop exactly halfway, which is where 1/2 is.
So 2/4 and 1/2 are equivalent — same place, two names.

problems.realWorld: Half a sandwich is the same as two quarter-pieces of that sandwich.

problems.question: On a line cut into thirds, where does 3/3 land?

steps.explanation: The denominator 3 means 3 equal pieces make one whole.
The numerator 3 means you walk all 3 pieces.
Walking every piece brings you to the whole, which is 1.

problems.realWorld: Eating all three slices of a three-slice pie means you ate one whole pie.

problems.question: A line is cut into fourths and continues past 1. Where does 5/4 land?

steps.explanation: Four fourths get you exactly to 1.
The fifth fourth is one more piece beyond that.
So 5/4 sits one fourth to the right of 1.

problems.realWorld: Running one full lap plus a quarter of the next one.

problems.question: Order these from smallest to largest: 3/4, 1/4, 2/4.

steps.explanation: All three have the same denominator, so the pieces are the same size.
When piece size matches, more pieces means farther from 0.
Counting up the numerators gives 1/4, then 2/4, then 3/4.

problems.realWorld: Three water bottles filled to different quarter marks, lined up shortest to tallest.

history.title: Bigger, Smaller, or Equal to One-Half?
Check Your Thinking

questions.question: What does the DENOMINATOR of a fraction tell you?
options: How many equal pieces the whole was cut into
How many pieces you are counting
How far the fraction is from zero
Whether the fraction is big or small
questions.correctAnswer: How many equal pieces the whole was cut into

questions.question: On a number line from 0 to 1 cut into 8 equal pieces, where is 4/8?
options: Exactly halfway between 0 and 1
Just before 1
Just after 0
Past 1

questions.question: Which is LARGER: 1/6 or 1/3?
options: 1/3, because fewer pieces means bigger pieces
1/6, because 6 is a bigger number than 3
They are equal because both numerators are 1
You cannot compare them without a calculator
questions.correctAnswer: 1/3, because fewer pieces means bigger pieces

questions.question: The reading says 3/4 is a location, not a pizza slice. What does that mean?
options: It is a specific spot on the number line, three-fourths of the way from 0 to 1
It only works for measuring distance
Pizza cannot be used to show fractions
It means 3/4 is not really a number
questions.correctAnswer: It is a specific spot on the number line, three-fourths of the way from 0 to 1

questions.question: Your classmate says '1/8 must be bigger than 1/4 because 8 is bigger than 4.' Draw or describe a number line that shows why this is wrong.
questions.expectedAnswer: Cut 0 to 1 into 4 pieces and mark 1/4; then cut the same line into 8 pieces and mark 1/8. The 1/8 mark is closer to 0. More pieces make each piece smaller, so 1/4 is larger.

questions.question: Explain in your own words what makes two fractions equivalent.
questions.expectedAnswer: They land on the exact same spot on the number line — the same amount with two different names, like 2/4 and 1/2.

reflections.text: Where do you see fractions outside of math class? Name two real places.
Which was easier for you: thinking of fractions as slices or as places on a line? Why?

history.meta: 5 MCQ + 2 short answer • Q3 is the classic reversal

items.text: The denominator ____ tells me the whole was cut into ____ equal pieces.
To find ____ on the number line, I start at 0 and walk ____ pieces.
I know ____ is bigger than ____ because ____.
When the denominator gets bigger, each piece gets ____, because ____.
A fraction is a number because ____.

data.rubric:
| Criteria | 1 — Starting | 3 — Getting There | 5 — Mathematician |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vocabulary | Says top and bottom number | Uses numerator or denominator | Uses both terms correctly every time |
| Reasoning | States the answer only | Gives one reason | Explains using pieces or the number line |
| Representation | No drawing | Draws a line | Draws a labeled line that matches the explanation |
